items.title: June 16, 1988: Isiah Thomas and Joe Dumars give Detroit a 10-point lead on the Lakers with 5:19 to go in Game 5 of the NBA Finals, in front of the largest crowd in NBA playoff history (41,732 in the Pontiac Silverdome).
items.author: NBA Cobwebs
items.body_excerpt:
June 16, 1988: Isiah Thomas and Joe Dumars give Detroit a 10-point lead on the Lakers with 5:19 to go in Game 5 of the NBA Finals, in front of the largest crowd in NBA playoff history (41,732 in the Pontiac Silverdome).

The Pistons won, 104-94, and took a 3-2 series lead.

items.title: Key takeaway
items.author: Tom Ziller
items.body_excerpt: Key takeaway

images.alt:
"If the NBA's richest owner can apparently secretly funnel $50 million to his best player above and beyond his NBA salary, get caught and get away with it without real sanction, there is no reason for any other rich owner to follow the rules. And where does that leave us?

Well, then the NBA has a strict salary cap on paper that regulates player salary and team cap structures and actually doesn't exist in reality. In reality, under the surface, it will have well-resourced teams funneling extra money to key players to sign with those teams, or take below-market deals, or execute this or that option. It will be a league that pats itself on the back for competitive balance and parity while hiding the real league, where the richest owners find creative ways to pay players under the table through pass-through fake endorsement deals or investments. Every contract and signing, especially those below what is perceived as market value, will carry a shroud of suspicion.

Maybe that's already the league we have, and the veil of legitimacy is just being peeled back for a peek into the underbelly because Ballmer was dumb enough to run his scam through a company that was itself an enormous scam. Whether that's the case, or if Ballmer is outlier, if he is not made an example here, it is open season on cap circumvention, which means that for all intents and purposes, the salary cap is fake, just like the trees that Aspiration said it was going to plant."

items.title: Probably not what people want to read about during the week between a great Finals and a great draft, but we're going to soon find out whether the NBA's salary cap rules are as fake as the trees Kawhi Leonard was supposed to plant www.teamziller.com/clippers-ste...
